chunks.principle: The best golf courses are built from three schools of design: penal (hazards punish poor shots), strategic (hazards create choices about line of play), and heroic (hazards reward bold carries). Most great courses borrow from all three.
chunks.application: Identify which school each hazard belongs to. Strategic hazards always leave a safe route — find it. Heroic hazards offer risk-reward — only attempt the carry if you know your actual carry distance, not your best-ever distance.

chunks.principle: The most engaging courses lure players in with tempting tee shots but always leave a safer route. A well-designed hole makes the golfer plan backward from the green to determine where the drive should land.
chunks.application: Before pulling driver, look at the green and work backward. Which side gives the best approach angle? Where do the fairway hazards sit relative to your carry distance? The tee shot is the first move in a chess game, not a stand-alone power play.

chunks.principle: A course you could play every day and never tire of offers playability for all skill levels and enough thought-provoking moments to keep it compelling. The greatest courses let you win now and then — they don't just punish.
chunks.application: Approach each hole looking for what the architect gave you, not just what threatens you. There is always a 'way around' designed for the golfer who plays within their means.

chunks.principle: Google Earth and satellite imagery let any golfer scout a course before playing. Studying the aerial view reveals the width of greens, the location of bunkers, and safe bail-out areas that ground-level views can hide.
chunks.application: Before playing a new course, spend 10 minutes on Google Earth noting where greens have open approaches, where bunkers guard the sides, and where the safe miss is on each hole. Don't stress over forced carries — they look worse from above than at ground level.

chunks.principle: Heroic hazards laid on a diagonal offer every player the thrill of carrying their own portion — the longer hitter can attempt more, but even the short hitter has a meaningful choice. The irregular hazard edge forces honesty about your own abilities.
chunks.application: When facing a diagonal hazard, choose the carry distance that gives you at least a 15-yard margin over your average carry — not your best. The architect wants the gambler to lose and the honest self-assessor to succeed.

chunks.principle: Courses designed mainly to resist scoring eventually bore even good players. The most beloved courses in the world — St Andrews, Cypress Point, North Berwick — give players moments of triumph alongside their challenges.
chunks.application: Don't measure a course by how hard it is. Measure it by whether you had fun figuring it out. A bogey on a well-played strategic hole is more satisfying than a par on a boring one.

chunks.principle: If you can't remember every hole after a round, the design may lack variety and strategic interest. Memorable holes feature 'sticky' details — surprising contours, risk-reward choices, or beautiful settings that lodge in your memory.
chunks.application: After your round, replay each hole in your mind. The holes you remember clearly are the ones where you made an interesting strategic choice. Learn from those moments — they reveal what kind of course management works for you.

chunks.principle: Many great par-3 holes are designed with surroundings that distort depth perception, making the green look smaller or farther than it is. The fear of the setting often causes more damage than the hazards themselves.
chunks.application: On par 3s, trust your yardage over your eyes. The visual drama is designed to intimidate. Pick the center of the green, choose the right club based on actual distance, and commit to the swing.

chunks.principle: On par 5s, the architect often places the most interesting strategic choices on the second shot — whether to lay up or go for it, and where to position for the best approach angle. The tee shot is usually more forgiving.
chunks.application: On par 5s, don't autopilot to 'hit it as far as possible.' Your second shot determines everything. If bunkers guard the green at your second-shot distance, find the layup yardage that avoids them and leaves your favorite wedge distance.

chunks.principle: Wind is one of the oldest strategic elements in golf, present on every links course since the game began. Firm ground and wind together create conditions where controlling trajectory and using the bounce become essential skills.
chunks.application: In wind, take more club and swing easier rather than swinging harder with your normal club. A controlled 6-iron in a headwind is far more reliable than a muscled 8-iron. The wind magnifies every spin and curve in your shot.

chunks.principle: Cart paths and other man-made additions sometimes negate the architect's original intent. A drive that bounces off concrete and out of bounds, for instance, negates the entire strategic point of a hole.
chunks.application: When a hazard seems unfair or random, it might not be part of the original design. Focus on the natural hazards and terrain features — those reveal the architect's intended strategy for the hole.

chunks.principle: Many courses feature understated 'change-of-pace' holes — shorter or easier holes designed to give golfers a breather before a tough finishing stretch. These holes are strategic opportunities, not throwaways.
chunks.application: Recognize the easier holes as scoring opportunities. Don't get careless on them — play your normal strategy. A par or birdie on a breather hole puts you in better shape for the challenging stretch ahead.

chunks.principle: Greens generally look tinier from aerial views than they really are, and trouble can look more menacing than it is. Tree-lined fairways appear narrower from above. Don't let pre-round scouting spike your anxiety.
chunks.application: Use aerial imagery for logistics and strategy, not for building fear. Note the safe areas, plan your preferred lines, but trust that the course will feel more spacious and forgiving at ground level.

chunks.principle: Professional golfers' tactics rarely apply to recreational players. Pros play a fundamentally different game with custom-fit equipment and extreme clubhead speed. Their course management is designed for their 300-yard drives.
chunks.application: Stop trying to play like Tour pros. Your strategy should be built around your actual distances and shot patterns. A 200-yard drive to the center of the fairway often produces better scores than a 250-yard drive into trouble.

chunks.principle: The value of severe hazards — like deep pot bunkers — is to test whether you'll admit your mistake and chip out, or compound the error trying to recover. Bobby Clampett lost the 1982 Open by repeatedly refusing to take his medicine from pot bunkers.
chunks.application: When you're in a deep bunker or bad lie, always ask: 'Am I trying to recover, or am I trying to be a hero?' Take the safe escape, accept the bogey, and save yourself from a double or worse.
